title: Shapiro steps of superfluid Fermi gases in a ring trap across the BCS--BEC crossover
authors: Hikaru Kuriki, Masaya Kunimi, Tetsuro Nikuni
abstract: We investigate the transport properties of a superfluid Fermi gas confined in a ring trap with a moving potential barrier across the Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) to Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) crossover. Employing time-dependent Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) equations, we simulate the dynamics of a Josephson junction biased by both DC and AC currents. Over a wide range of interaction strengths, we observe clear low-order Shapiro-step plateaus in the barrier-velocity--chemical-potential-diffenrence, within the phase-coherent regime, where the time-averaged chemical potential difference is quantized in units of $\hbarω/2$. This factor of $1/2$ reflects our convention of defining the chemical potential per single fermion in the BdG framework. Microscopic analysis reveals that these fundamental steps originate from synchronized phase slips mediated by periodic soliton generation at the barrier. Our findings clarify the role of interaction regimes in the nonequilibrium phase dynamics of ring-trapped fermionic superfluids and provide microscopic insights relevant to future studies of atomtronic systems with nontrivial topology.
summary: This paper explores how Shapiro steps emerge in a driven superfluid Fermi gas in a ring trap. It demonstrates that these quantized steps are caused by the synchronization of soliton emissions at a moving barrier. The findings provide a microscopic understanding of transport properties across the BCS-BEC crossover.
main_problem: Investigating the nonequilibrium dynamics and transport properties of Shapiro steps in a superfluid Fermi gas within a ring trap across the BCS-BEC crossover.
main_result: The study observes quantized Shapiro-step plateaus in the chemical potential difference, which originate from synchronized phase slips mediated by periodic soliton generation at the barrier.
method: Numerical simulations using the time-dependent Bogoliable-de Gennes (tdBdG) equations solved via the split-step Fourier method and a modified Broyden's method for self-consistency.
model_or_system: A superfluid Fermi gas in a 1D ring geometry containing a moving Gaussian potential barrier that acts as a Josephson junction biased by DC and AC currents.
key_observables: Chemical potential difference (delta-mu), topological winding number, order parameter amplitude and phase, local current density, and particle number density.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (kF a inverse), DC barrier velocity (v_DC), AC amplitude (x_AC), AC frequency (omega), and the Stewart-McCumber parameter (beta_c).
main_assumptions: The system is treated in an effective 1D geometry at zero temperature, and the midpoint order parameter is assumed to evolve smoothly during time steps.
figures_summary: Schematic of the ring trap setup; dynamics of the order parameter and winding number; relationship between barrier velocity and chemical potential difference showing Shapiro steps; and the evolution of local velocity at the barrier.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical setup and problem, details the numerical BdG framework and self-consistent loop, presents results on the superflow-to-voltage transition, analyzes the microscopic soliton-mediated mechanism of Shapiro steps, and discusses the breakdown in the BCS regime.
why_interesting: It provides microscopic insights into phase-locking and nonequilibrium phase dynamics in topological atomtronic systems, bridging the gap between fundamental BCS-BEC crossover physics and driven Josephson junction dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of a ring-trapped Fermi super- fluid with a moving Gaussian potential barrier V (x, t) under combined DC and AC driving (blue shaded area). The barrier position xb(t) is defined in Eq. (2). x0 is the initial position of the barrier, and xf = xb(tf) is the final position at the end of the simulation. The point opposite to the barrier position is used as an effective boundary to define the left and right reservoirs, which are separated by the barrier and its antipode (black dotted line). NL and NR represent the atom numbers in the left and right reservoirs, respectively.
FIG. 2. Relationship between the DC barrier velocity vDC and the chemical potential difference ∆µ(tf) across the BCS– BEC crossover.

Figure 2 shows the relationship between the DC bar- rier velocity vDC normalized by the Fermi velocity vF = ℏkF/m and the chemical potential difference ∆µ(tf) cal- culated from a linear fit (see Sec. II A) for various cou- pling strengths (kFa)−1. We observe a critical velocity vc below which ∆µ remains zero, indicating a superflow.

FIG. 3. (a) Capacitance C, (b) conductance G, (c) Stewart- McCumber parameter βc, and (d) critical velocity vc ex- tracted from the RCSJ model as a function of the interac- tion strength (kFa)−1. The dimensionless capacitance CEF is calculated from the charging energy EC as C = E−1 C (see Appendix B for details).

FIG. 4. Dynamics of the order parameter ∆(x, t) in the UFG regime [(kFa)−1 = 0] at vDC = 0.137vF, which is above the critical velocity vc (i.e., in the voltage regime). (a) Amplitude |∆(x, t)| and (b) phase arg ∆(x, t) of the order parameter as functions of position x and time t. Note that the potential barrier appears stationary in these plots because the dynamics are calculated in the frame co-moving with the DC velocity vDC. The nearly vertical depletion near the barrier corresponds to the suppression of the order parameter by the barrier, while the slanted depletion structures propagating away from it correspond to emitted solitons.
